category_summary:
Open-model momentum and recursive self-improvement dominated today's discourse. **NVIDIA** headlined with **Nemotron 3 Ultra**, [a fully open 550B MoE](/?date=2026-06-05&category=social#item-d94919b2b56a) built for long-running agents, touting a hybrid Mamba-Transformer architecture, ~5x faster inference, and [day-0 vLLM support](/?date=2026-06-05&category=social#item-01255f7a80f9). **NVIDIA** also unveiled **Cosmos 3**, [an open omni-model for physical AI](/?date=2026-06-05&category=social#item-d82880792044).

- **Anthropic's** viral 'When AI builds itself' thread [claimed Claude is accelerating](/?date=2026-06-05&category=social#item-35e4a3828332) its own development, citing 80%+ of merged code authored by Claude, 8x engineer output, and a coding-speedup test [jumping from 3x to ~52x](/?date=2026-06-05&category=social#item-dd1f8ef3ef1f) with **Mythos Preview**. **Ethan Mollick** [amplified the 80% code figure](/?date=2026-06-05&category=social#item-114877fc9d06) while noting a mix of sincere belief and marketing.
- **OpenAI** announced a major **ChatGPT memory** upgrade with [cross-conversation context](/?date=2026-06-05&category=social#item-57a5cb83c141), user-steerable summaries, and 2x capacity, drawing high engagement and privacy discussion.
- **Gary Marcus** struck a bearish note, predicting **OpenAI** [faces deep financial trouble](/?date=2026-06-05&category=social#item-f9b5eff14821) and possible acquisition by **Microsoft** or **Amazon**.

- On governance, an open letter signed by **Altman**, **Amodei**, **Hassabis**, and Nobel laureates [urged mandatory synthetic DNA screening](/?date=2026-06-05&category=social#item-54ded00566de) amid claims AI now outperforms PhD virologists.
- **Nathan Lambert** highlighted **NVIDIA** [adopting multi-teacher on-policy distillation](/?date=2026-06-05&category=social#item-6d462d4ffb70) as the new post-training standard, and **Andrew Ng** [launched a course on efficient LLM serving](/?date=2026-06-05&category=social#item-b5c2fa9075f8) with vLLM.

  • Anthropic's viral 'When AI builds itself' thread claimed Claude is accelerating its own development, citing 80%+ of merged code authored by Claude, 8x engineer output, and a coding-speedup test jumping from 3x to ~52x with Mythos Preview.   • On governance, an open letter signed by Altman, Amodei, Hassabis, and Nobel laureates urged mandatory synthetic DNA screening amid claims AI now outperforms PhD virologists.

items.title:
OpenAI is in deep, deep trouble 

They are low on capital relative to the massive cash they are burn...
items.content:
OpenAI is in deep, deep trouble 

They are low on capital relative to the massive cash they are burning and there is only so much capital in the world. 

No rational person would sell their Bitcoin or Nvidia stock or whatever to buy OpenAI rather than Anthropic. 

And nobody is going to acquire OpenAI for a trillion dollars.

Instead, my guess is that they get absorbed by Microsoft or Amazon, at something  like 30 cents on the dollar.

items.title: Each time we release a model, we run the same test: give it code that trains a small AI model, ask t...
items.content:
Each time we release a model, we run the same test: give it code that trains a small AI model, ask the new model to speed it up. It takes a skilled human 4-8 hours to reach 4x faster.

In May 2024, Claude Opus 4 averaged a ~3x speedup. This April, Mythos Preview achieved ~52x.

items.title: We’re building ChatGPT to remember what matters, follow your preferences and constraints, and adapt ...
items.content:
We’re building ChatGPT to remember what matters, follow your preferences and constraints, and adapt as things change.

If you tell ChatGPT you’re planning a trip in July, memory should understand when the trip is upcoming, happening, and already over.

That helps ChatGPT keep giving relevant answers as context changes.

items.title:
"AI systems now outperform PhD-level virologists."

Sam Altman and Dario Amodei don't agree on much,...
items.content:
"AI systems now outperform PhD-level virologists."

Sam Altman and Dario Amodei don't agree on much, but this week they signed the same letter.

Also on the list: Demis Hassabis, Alexandr Wang, Mustafa Suleyman, several Nobel laureates, and the DNA-synthesis industry itself.

The open letter calls on Congress to make screening of synthetic DNA orders mandatory, along with the machines that print them, and to require recordkeeping so any order can be traced.

The warning: "there is a real possibility that the knowledge barriers which have historically prevented bad actors from obtaining biological weapons will meaningfully erode."
items.author: @TheRundownAI
items.summary: The Rundown AI reports on an open letter signed by Altman, Amodei, Hassabis, Wang, Suleyman, and Nobel laureates urging Congress to mandate synthetic DNA order screening, warning AI now outperforms PhD virologists and biosecurity barriers may erode.
items.reasoning: Significant biosecurity policy news with rare consensus among rival AI leaders; high relevance to AI safety and governance, moderate engagement.

items.title: New course on serving LLMs efficiently -- how do you serve models to many concurrent users at low la...
items.content:
New course on serving LLMs efficiently -- how do you serve models to many concurrent users at low latency and reasonable cost? This short course is built with  @RedHat and taught by @cedricclyburn.

Efficient LLM serving requires efficient memory management. A 70B-parameter model takes ~140 GB just to load the weights. On top of that, every active request needs its own chunk of GPU memory, the KV cache, to store the token context it has built up so far. In this course, you'll learn to reduce a model's memory footprint with quantization and serve it using vLLM, which handles many concurrent requests efficiently through smart memory management.

Skills you'll gain:
- Quantize a model and measure the accuracy tradeoff
- Serve a model with vLLM and watch it handle concurrent requests efficiently
- Benchmark your deployment and make informed tradeoffs between speed, cost, and accuracy

Join and learn to serve LLMs efficiently:
https://t.co/x04xMbFlkO

items.title: "We believe it would be good for the world to have the option to slow or temporarily pause frontier ...
items.content:
"We believe it would be good for the world to have the option to slow or temporarily pause frontier AI development."

A jarring quote from Anthropic in today's 'When AI builds itself' post on recursive self-improvement (RSI).

The piece lays out where the tech is heading: AI that autonomously designs and develops its own successor.

Anthropic shared the signs of RSI already being seen within the company. 

More than 80% of the code the lab merges is now written by Claude, up from low single digits prior to Claude Code launching in early 2025.

The typical engineer also ships 8x as much code per quarter as they used to. 

On a potential pause, Anthropic said the concern is coordination and actually proving rivals  across the globe have stopped development. 

The arms-control treaties that did this "took decades to build both the infrastructure and the trust."

"We don't have that long."

items.content_html:
"We believe it would be good for the world to have the option to slow or temporarily pause frontier AI development."

A jarring quote from Anthropic in today's 'When AI builds itself' post on recursive self-improvement (RSI).

The piece lays out where the tech is heading: AI that autonomously designs and develops its own successor.

Anthropic shared the signs of RSI already being seen within the company.

More than 80% of the code the lab merges is now written by Claude, up from low single digits prior to Claude Code launching in early 2025.

The typical engineer also ships 8x as much code per quarter as they used to.

On a potential pause, Anthropic said the concern is coordination and actually proving rivals across the globe have stopped development.

The arms-control treaties that did this "took decades to build both the infrastructure and the trust."

"We don't have that long."

items.title: Watch the goal post shift unfold in real time: AGI used to be doing anything a person, including an ...
items.content:
Watch the goal post shift unfold in real time: AGI used to be doing anything a person, including an expert, could do, and by the end of the interview it’s not.

I call this the AI bait and switch. As I wrote last year:

“AI hype has become a giant game of bait and switch.

the bait: we are going to make an AI that can solve any problem a expert human could solve. it’s gonna transform the whole world.

the switch: what we have actually made is fun and kind of amazing in its own way but rarely reliable and often makes mistakes – but ordinary people makes mistakes, too. So … AGI solved!”
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Gary Marcus accuses the AI field of goalpost-shifting on AGI definitions, describing it as a bait-and-switch in hype narratives.
items.reasoning: Well-articulated recurring critique with solid engagement from a leading skeptic; captures an active discourse theme.

items.title:
Three Predictions: 

1. Some form of AI, probably neurosymbolic in nature, will come that is far mor...
items.content:
Three Predictions: 

1. Some form of AI, probably neurosymbolic in nature, will come that is far more economical and data- and energy-efficient than LLMs, and it will make an absolute fortune.

2. LLMs, on the other hand, will never be all that profitable (aside from the chip companies selling shovels in the gold rush).

3. Today’s gigantic bets are premature, and most won’t pay off.

items.title: 🎉 The vLLM community just got a free course, built by @RedHat_AI with @DeepLearningAI. It walks thro...
items.content:
🎉 The vLLM community just got a free course, built by @RedHat_AI with @DeepLearningAI. It walks through the full optimize → deploy → benchmark lifecycle for serving open models.

Three labs, each on a live vLLM server:
- Compress: quantize a Qwen model with LLM Compressor, then measure the size vs. accuracy tradeoff
- Serve: deploy with vLLM's OpenAI-compatible API and watch continuous batching, PagedAttention, and prefix caching in the live metrics
- Benchmark: simulate traffic with GuideLLM and check quality with lm-eval

A lot of the work went into visualizing what actually happens under inference, thanks to @cedricclyburn: how tokens flow through the model, how the KV cache grows in GPU memory, and what changes when you move from FP16 to INT8/INT4.

~1.5 hours, 9 lessons, 3 labs. Free on https://t.co/pGAwqbmMdc.

📝 Read more: https://t.co/r8ITc2prI2

A lot of the work went into visualizing what actually happens under inference, thanks to @cedricclyburn: how tokens flow through the model, how the KV cache grows in GPU memory, and what changes when you move from FP16 to INT8/INT4.

items.title:
We're presenting ParseBench at CVPR 2026! 

ParseBench is the most comprehensive document understand...
items.content:
We're presenting ParseBench at CVPR 2026! 

ParseBench is the most comprehensive document understanding benchmark for VLMs. 
✅ It contains 2k pages of real-world enterprise documents
✅ It has comprehensive evaluation metrics around tables, charts, visual grounding, semantic formatting, and content faithfulness

The core goal is measuring whether models can semantically interpret a document in the right way, without having models overfit to our precise benchmark. 

Parsing 100% of PDFs to 100% accuracy is the final boss for document OCR. 
In general, the latest frontier models have been tuned for coding, math, and scientific reasoning as opposed to precise visual understanding; hope more benchmarks that these will encourage overall progress towards solving this problem! 

Poster is below. If you want to learn more come check out our site or 30-page ArXiv paper: 

ParseBench: https://t.co/PWczfhp0OX
ArXiv: https://t.co/2dEJIaBBkr

Proud to announce that Cohere has been awarded first place in NATO’s Agentic AI for Cognitive Warfare Innovation Challenge.

Congratulations to our fellow finalists: OpenMinds, which secured second place, and Ipsos and Thoughtworks, which shared third.

The competition highlighted the growing role of agentic AI in helping democratic nations understand, anticipate and respond to information threats. We're honoured to have our work recognised by NATO and proud to be contributing technology that strengthens decision-making and resilience across the Alliance.

items.title: ⚠️Why didn’t the hyperscalers wait until after the IPO in switching to pay-by-usage-charging? My gue...
items.content:
⚠️Why didn’t the hyperscalers wait until after the IPO in switching to pay-by-usage-charging? My guess is that *they literally could not afford to* —because it would bankrupt them. 

My reasoning: in “all you can eat”  agent era they were hemorrhaging money. 

So the hyperscalers probably had to choose between hemorrhaging insane amounts of money (with tokenmaxxing) and reducing usage and potentially burning customer enthusiasm (via increasing rates).

They would eat another 6 months of all you can eat if they possibly could– to establish market appetite—so I conclude that they just can’t. 

Conclusion: They just don’t have enough cash. Despite the possibility of giant IPOs, their financial situation is shaky.

items.title: So, I use Codex as an integral part of ChapterPal. Codex is installed in a Google Cloud Run containe...
items.content:
So, I use Codex as an integral part of ChapterPal. Codex is installed in a Google Cloud Run container, and my monthly subscription is used when it's invoked.

Periodically, Codex's authentication token expires, so I run an sh script that updates this token in production so that Codex keeps running.

Today, there was a bug, and I asked Codex (my local one) to investigate and fix it.

The retard figured out that the authentication token expired and instead of asking me to run the sh script (or running it itself), it changed the app's code so that Codex in the Cloud Run now uses an OpenAI API key with pay-per-token pricing.

Remember, these things cannot be trusted. It's exactly the same situation as when they say that it's better to walk to the car wash instead of driving there. They are disconnected from reality and will never become connected because of the way they were built.

These "agents" aren't agents. They are dumb motherfuckers that will do the dumbest things that will leave you penniless unless you watch them every single second.

By the way, I don't see any moron with a🦞emoji in my feed. I have an idea why.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov recounts how Codex misdiagnosed an expired auth token and rewrote app code to use a pay-per-token API key, arguing agents are unreliable and disconnected from reality.
items.reasoning: Concrete real-world failure anecdote about coding agent reliability from a known practitioner; good engagement and a relevant cautionary tale.
items.summary_html: Burkov recounts how Codex misdiagnosed an expired auth token and rewrote app code to use a pay-per-token API key, arguing agents are unreliable and disconnected from reality.
items.content_html:
So, I use Codex as an integral part of ChapterPal. Codex is installed in a Google Cloud Run container, and my monthly subscription is used when it's invoked.

Periodically, Codex's authentication token expires, so I run an sh script that updates this token in production so that Codex keeps running.

Today, there was a bug, and I asked Codex (my local one) to investigate and fix it.

The retard figured out that the authentication token expired and instead of asking me to run the sh script (or running it itself), it changed the app's code so that Codex in the Cloud Run now uses an OpenAI API key with pay-per-token pricing.

Remember, these things cannot be trusted. It's exactly the same situation as when they say that it's better to walk to the car wash instead of driving there. They are disconnected from reality and will never become connected because of the way they were built.

These "agents" aren't agents. They are dumb motherfuckers that will do the dumbest things that will leave you penniless unless you watch them every single second.

By the way, I don't see any moron with a🦞emoji in my feed. I have an idea why.

items.title:
1. DGX Station with GB300 superchip and up to 748GB of memory. The numbers here are pretty insane.

...
items.content:
1. DGX Station with GB300 superchip and up to 748GB of memory. The numbers here are pretty insane.

2. RTX Spark laptops with 1 petaflop of AI performance and 128GB of unified memory. This is the one I'm the most excited about!

DGX Station and RTX Spark ← These 2 alone are really exciting! You will be able to run trillion of parameters in your personal hardware. We are talking about frontier models.

But there's even more:

3. OpenShell is now in GitHub Copilot. This brings you sandboxing and policy-as-code, which are huge for agentic systems.

4. Claude now runs natively on NVIDIA's GB300 systems on Azure.

5. Nemotron 3 Ultra is out. This is an open frontier reasoning model built for long-running agents.

They kept announcing things, and the list is too long, so here is the blog post with everything:

https://t.co/lnuCfqPad6

Thanks to the Microsoft and NVIDIA teams for partnering with me on this post.

items.title: @provisionalidea I dunno what to tell you. I published a lot of survey research in peer reviewed jou...
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Ethan Mollick critiques the NANDA report methodology as deceptive and far below social-science standards, defending his expertise.
items.reasoning: Detailed methodological critique of a widely-cited AI adoption study from a credible researcher, though low engagement on this reply.
items.summary_html: Ethan Mollick critiques the NANDA report methodology as deceptive and far below social-science standards, defending his expertise.
items.content_html:
@provisionalidea I dunno what to tell you. I published a lot of survey research in peer reviewed journals and Toby is one of the most cited sociologists in the world. From my perspective as an expert, what NANDA provided is deceptive in terms of methodology and the research notes make things worse, not better -- they are so far from what the standards in the field are. You can argue with me if you want, but I will claim some expertise here, yet I can't make you believe it.

To be clear, I don't even disagree with the conclusions that AI pilots were failing in mid-2025 (or today)! I do not like that the results of bad research methodologies in a one-off report are being shared widely as if they were done by MIT researchers using the standards of social science (as a PhD from there, I find it especially irksome). If it wasn't a commonly cited study by the media, I wouldn't bother trying to debunk it.

Anyway, I am not sure where to go from here. You want to go to the mat for the NANDA study, that is fine. We agree on the substance of the situation in 2025 beyond our views of the study.

items.title:
A brief history of claims about AI and employment

2016: Hinton said radiologists would soon be repl...
items.content:
A brief history of claims about AI and employment

2016: Hinton said radiologists would soon be replaced; they weren’t. He was hyping his tech, not basing it on careful analysis of the industry.

2026: Amodei said white collar labor would soon be replaced; it hasn’t been. He was hyping his tech, not basing it on careful analysis of any particular industry.

2026: Amodei walked it back when people got mad.  He was trying to downplay public concerns, not basing it on careful analysis of any particular industry.

items.summary: Marcus draws a parallel between Hinton's 2016 radiologist-replacement claim and Amodei's white-collar automation claims, framing both as hype rather than industry analysis.
items.reasoning: Good engagement and a substantive historical argument about repeated overhyping of AI labor impacts.

2016: Hinton said radiologists would soon be replaced; they weren’t. He was hyping his tech, not basing it on careful analysis of the industry.

2026: Amodei said white collar labor would soon be replaced; it hasn’t been. He was hyping his tech, not basing it on careful analysis of any particular industry.

2026: Amodei walked it back when people got mad. He was trying to downplay public concerns, not basing it on careful analysis of any particular industry.

items.title:
The way we do research is about to change.

Today, doing research is overwhelming. There's so much l...
items.content:
The way we do research is about to change.

Today, doing research is overwhelming. There's so much literature to review and so little time that picking the wrong path could take you back years.

We might have a solution for this:

There's a new multi-agent system with four specialized agents to do AI research at scale:

Ideation → Planning → Experiment → Writing

Lemma's FARS (Fully Automated Research System) mode was built to produce hypothesis-validation pairs so that a single researcher can explore in a week what used to take a lab a year.

Their four agents can run a full research loop end-to-end without relying on humans at all.

Each agent owns one stage of the pipeline:

• The Ideation agent finds the hypothesis
• The Planning agent designs the study
• The Experiment agent runs it
• The Writing agent drafts the paper

This design allows FARS to run multiple research threads in parallel, like a lab with dozens of researchers working simultaneously.

I read their blog post, and the way they architected the system is pretty interesting:

The agents don't talk to each other directly. Instead, they coordinate through a shared file system that doubles as a workspace and a persistent memory.

This allows the system to scale to many concurrent projects.

Here is what FARS gives you:

You give it a research direction → The system generates hypotheses → Runs them through automated reviews → The ones that pass move down the pipeline → You get back short papers focused on a single, well-scoped contribution, each one is a hypothesis and its validation.

items.title:
I think a new metric should be $/Mt

> money you made per million tokens

So let's say you make $2,0...
items.content:
I think a new metric should be $/Mt

> money you made per million tokens

So let's say you make $2,000/mo and spend 10 million tokens to do that

That's $200/Mt 
($200 made per million tokens)

Because you have a lot of people just spending lots of tokens but it's often performative and they don't even produce anything with it

This way you can see the most efficient people who can convert AI tokens into actual $$$

items.title:
Good morning AI and robot fans. (Which really are the same thing).

Did you miss how big a day yeste...
items.content:
Good morning AI and robot fans. (Which really are the same thing).

Did you miss how big a day yesterday was in AI? https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ didn't.

You saw this coming, didn't you? @cixliv did.

While I got you, I am not paid by @TownAI (a new system that watches everything I do and helps us) but it was the big winner of yesterday for me. And that's saying something, because yesterday was so huge.

And last night had dinner with @Sanjil, who is building a new agentic simulator and infrastructure for people to take robots to the next level. 

It'll be out in a couple of months, but he educated me a lot on just how hard it is to get robots from doing a few tasks, like these Unitree robots are doing, as impressive as the tasks are, to being truly generalized where it can do thousands, or even millions of tasks. 

Better ways to teach robots to do a lot more than dance coming soon.

Did you miss how big a day yesterday was in AI? https://t.co/8L5xphk0qQ didn't.

You saw this coming, didn't you? @cixliv did.

While I got you, I am not paid by @TownAI (a new system that watches everything I do and helps us) but it was the big winner of yesterday for me. And that's saying something, because yesterday was so huge.

And last night had dinner with @Sanjil, who is building a new agentic simulator and infrastructure for people to take robots to the next level.

It'll be out in a couple of months, but he educated me a lot on just how hard it is to get robots from doing a few tasks, like these Unitree robots are doing, as impressive as the tasks are, to being truly generalized where it can do thousands, or even millions of tasks.

Better ways to teach robots to do a lot more than dance coming soon.

items.title: I think @Levie is overstating the positive case for employment in the (near term) AI era but that mo...
items.content:
I think @Levie is overstating the positive case for employment in the (near term) AI era but that most people have overstated the negative case, and that the truth is somewhere in between. 

Which is to say there will be some new jobs, some lost jobs—and that the overall net effect won’t be huge in the next few years.

My prediction for 2025 (which proved to be correct) was “Less than 10% of the work force will be replaced by AI. Probably less than 5%”.

I will stand by that prediction for 2026, as well. 

cc @DavidSacks

items.title:
Is it possible to spot a good forecast by its rationale?

We used LLMs to score the reasoning behind...
items.content:
Is it possible to spot a good forecast by its rationale?

We used LLMs to score the reasoning behind 55,000+ forecasts and test the link between forecast accuracy and written rationales.

We found that:

• Causal reasoning is much more prevalent than statistical argumentation
• It's easier to identify poor forecasters rather than excellent ones
• Human ratings of rationale quality can be unreliable.

🧵A thread on the results:
items.author: @Research_FRI
items.summary: FRI's thread opener: using LLMs to score 55,000+ forecast rationales, finding causal reasoning dominates, poor forecasters are easier to spot, and human quality ratings can be unreliable.
items.reasoning: Strong lead post summarizing a credible study using LLMs for evaluation, with the thread's best engagement; relevant to AI-assisted evaluation.

• Causal reasoning is much more prevalent than statistical argumentation

• It's easier to identify poor forecasters rather than excellent ones

• Human ratings of rationale quality can be unreliable.

🧵A thread on the results:

items.title: 🏆 Round 2 of judging the Vibe Jam of 2026 sponsored  by @cursor_ai  + @boltdotnew + @heyglif + @trip...
items.content:
🏆 Round 2 of judging the Vibe Jam of 2026 sponsored  by @cursor_ai  + @boltdotnew + @heyglif + @tripoai is finished now

After going through 945 submissions in Round 1, me and @s13k_ then rated the top 100+ submissions in Round 2 with a rating from 0 to 10, trying the games extensively

Judging took a while because I wanted to really try the games and give all a chance, which took many hours, I did a lot of judging on a bicycle in the gym like in the pic below 😅

Anyway, now the Top 25 best games from those will go on to Round 3 and be judged by the final external judges:
- @timsoret from @oddtalesgames 
- @ericzakariasson from @cursor_ai 
- @NicolaManzini from VibeSail
- Alessandro from @OverJumpRally

Prizes:
- 🥇 $25,000 to 1st place
- 🥈 $10,000 to 2nd place
- 🥉 $5,000 to 3d place

And there will be some extra non-money awards (like most popular, most played etc!)

Depending on the judges I think we can have the winners by next week!!!

items.title:
A developer just replaced your entire backend tooling stack with one open source tool. 

Every backe...
items.content:
A developer just replaced your entire backend tooling stack with one open source tool. 

Every backend project starts the same way.
 
You wire up an API, a queue, a scheduler, and observability. 

That's four tools before any business logic ships.
 
An open-source engine called iii (pronounced as "three eye") collapses all of it into one runtime.
 
It runs on three primitives:
 
> Function: a unit of work
> Trigger: what makes it run
> Worker: connects to the engine
 
Need a queue, sandbox, or agent in the stack?
 
One CLI command adds it. The new capability joins a live catalog instantly. 

Every other process is notified and can call it.
 
Agents use the same interface developers do.
 
The whole thing installs as a single binary. 

TypeScript, Python, and Rust are supported out of the box.
 
The repo just crossed 16K stars and is growing fast.
 
What happens when the backend has its React moment?

Every backend project starts the same way.

That's four tools before any business logic ships.

An open-source engine called iii (pronounced as "three eye") collapses all of it into one runtime.

Agents use the same interface developers do.

TypeScript, Python, and Rust are supported out of the box.

The repo just crossed 16K stars and is growing fast.

What happens when the backend has its React moment?

items.title:
You can now stop writing skill files for your agent by hand. 
 
Coding agents forget what you taught...
items.content:
You can now stop writing skill files for your agent by hand. 
 
Coding agents forget what you taught them last week. 

Every session burns tokens re-explaining your procedures.
 
CLAUDE.md helps one agent, AGENTS.md helps another. 

You maintain both yourself, learning on behalf of the machine.
 
Watchmen flips this whole arrangement. 

It is a local, open-source tool sitting behind your coding agents.
 
It watches what you do. 

The recurring work gets written to disk as runnable skill bundles.
 
That context follows you across tools, with four pieces worth calling out:
 
> One corpus across every agent
> Workspace briefs written automatically
> Retrospective hints in your statusLine
> Local dashboard for impact tracking
 
A 16-week card tracks median tool errors, prompts, and cost per session. 

You see the difference clearly.
 
How long before your agent stops asking the same questions?

items.title:
3️⃣ What looks good to humans isn't always what's accurate

We compared human ratings of rationales ...
items.content:
3️⃣ What looks good to humans isn't always what's accurate

We compared human ratings of rationales from the ACE tournament with our EQM scores for the same rationales to find out which ratings were a better predictor of forecast accuracy.

We found that EQM scores had a stronger correlation with forecast accuracy than human ratings.
items.author: @Research_FRI
items.summary: FRI reports their EQM scores predicted forecast accuracy better than human ratings did.
items.reasoning: Notable comparative finding on LLM-based rationale scoring; technically interesting but minimal engagement.
items.summary_html: FRI reports their EQM scores predicted forecast accuracy better than human ratings did.
items.content_html:
3️⃣ What looks good to humans isn't always what's accurate

We compared human ratings of rationales from the ACE tournament with our EQM scores for the same rationales to find out which ratings were a better predictor of forecast accuracy.

We found that EQM scores had a stronger correlation with forecast accuracy than human ratings.

items.title:
To figure this out, we took the following approach:

1) Defined 60 Explanation Quality Markers (EQMs...
items.content:
To figure this out, we took the following approach:

1) Defined 60 Explanation Quality Markers (EQMs): features like statistical or fact-based reasoning, guessing, confirmation bias, or extreme confidence.

2) Used an LLM to score rationales against each of the 60 EQMs.

3) Collapsed those 60 scores into a single composite number per rationale.

4) Correlated that composite score with forecasting accuracy.
items.author: @Research_FRI
items.summary: FRI describes a method defining 60 Explanation Quality Markers, scoring rationales with an LLM, collapsing into a composite, and correlating with accuracy.
items.reasoning: Clear methodological description of an LLM-driven evaluation framework; technically relevant though low engagement.
items.summary_html: FRI describes a method defining 60 Explanation Quality Markers, scoring rationales with an LLM, collapsing into a composite, and correlating with accuracy.
items.content_html:
To figure this out, we took the following approach:

1) Defined 60 Explanation Quality Markers (EQMs): features like statistical or fact-based reasoning, guessing, confirmation bias, or extreme confidence.

2) Used an LLM to score rationales against each of the 60 EQMs.

3) Collapsed those 60 scores into a single composite number per rationale.

4) Correlated that composite score with forecasting accuracy.

items.title: @karatademada I am being pickier with resharing things because resharing isn't as honored as it once...
items.content:
@karatademada I am being pickier with resharing things because resharing isn't as honored as it once was. Curation is not as important to the algorithm as it once was, and little social signals (saying hi to people and stuff like that) are not valued as much as they once were.

The system wants to be a newsfeed of news. I'm trying to work on getting better news on my feed and getting more original content, because that's what Elon has signaled that he wants on the system.

So, I guess I am changing what I am doing with X. I'm studying it at a very deep level. I have lists of about 60,000 different people in the tech industry. It is showing me that the platform is turning much more toward interests. If you catch an interest that's high, sometimes you get more followers, more engagement, and more reads than if you are posting about something that isn't.

For somebody like me who has a broad interest across the tech industry (for instance, from security to blockchain to robots to AI), the system doesn't like that. It wants us to focus on one thing.

I'm thinking about it a lot, and I might start up new accounts that just focus on very specific things. We'll see. It's a system in change, and it's changing so quickly that it's hard to figure out what to do long term to build a brand, build an audience, and take care of them.
items.author: @Scobleizer

items.reasoning: Substantive observation on platform algorithm dynamics affecting tech/AI content distribution; modest engagement, experienced commentator.

The system wants to be a newsfeed of news. I'm trying to work on getting better news on my feed and getting more original content, because that's what Elon has signaled that he wants on the system.

So, I guess I am changing what I am doing with X. I'm studying it at a very deep level. I have lists of about 60,000 different people in the tech industry. It is showing me that the platform is turning much more toward interests. If you catch an interest that's high, sometimes you get more followers, more engagement, and more reads than if you are posting about something that isn't.

For somebody like me who has a broad interest across the tech industry (for instance, from security to blockchain to robots to AI), the system doesn't like that. It wants us to focus on one thing.

I'm thinking about it a lot, and I might start up new accounts that just focus on very specific things. We'll see. It's a system in change, and it's changing so quickly that it's hard to figure out what to do long term to build a brand, build an audience, and take care of them.

items.title: Why Japanese companies do so many different things: The internal logic of the world’s strangest corp...
items.content:
Why Japanese companies do so many different things: The internal logic of the world’s strangest corporations

https://t.co/RZPPbtodLz

TL;DR

> you have a firm that has lots of lifetime employees who can’t be fired, and whose skills are tailored to what your firm needs rather than to a particular occupational category transferable to any employer

> the system only makes sense if the company is also insulated from outside pressure

> the Japan-style company, run by its employees and largely indifferent to the interests of shareholders, exists simply to continue existing

> And that basic impulse toward survival is why Japanese companies are so insistent on diversification. If you’ve made a commitment to keep people employed for life, then you need to create jobs for them if their current jobs stop making sense

> If you’re not very worried about profitability, and have lots of well-trained generalist employees, then it makes perfect sense to reinvest your company’s earnings by expanding into new industries

items.title: In ChapterPal, there's a WYSIWYG markdown editor. I asked Codex to add a feature allowing the user t...
items.content:
In ChapterPal, there's a WYSIWYG markdown editor. I asked Codex to add a feature allowing the user to drag and move a border between two table columns to change their relative widths.

Codex implemented it, and it works. Except that the underlying markdown doesn't change. So the fix was purely on the UI side, not in the markdown.

I asked what was the point of doing a UI-only change in a WYSI-fucking-WYG editor, and it said that I was absolutely right. It didn't make sense.

Remember, everything, like EVERYTHING an LLM agent decides to do is this kind of crap. It just happens sometimes that you don't notice it.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov recounts Codex implementing a UI-only column-resize feature in a WYSIWYG editor without updating the underlying markdown, using it to argue LLM agents constantly make such mistakes unnoticed.
items.reasoning: Concrete, detailed critique of agent reliability with moderate engagement.

items.title: its been such fun befriending Pari and seeing him completely reinvent his company for the agentic er...
items.content:
its been such fun befriending Pari and seeing him completely reinvent his company for the agentic era, WHILE having the most insanely stacked customer base I've ever seen in the hardest engineering domain of all. 

Pari is creating a category that is only obvious in retrospect (the best kind of obvious) after you've had to build any complex physical things from rockets to cars, managing thousands of tiny design and supply chain details any one of which can derail your entire year. 

what value do you put on that?

items.title:
Why was this?

As you can see below, human ratings correlate strongly with rationale length, but len...
items.content:
Why was this?

As you can see below, human ratings correlate strongly with rationale length, but length is essentially uncorrelated to forecast-level accuracy.

Human raters weren’t wrong directionally, but they appeared to place undue weight on some features, such as underweighting "red flags" like extreme confidence.
items.author: @Research_FRI
items.summary: FRI notes human ratings correlate with rationale length but length is uncorrelated with forecast accuracy, and humans underweight red flags like extreme confidence.
items.reasoning: Substantive research finding on LLM-scored forecasting, though low engagement as part of a thread.

items.title: @sd_marlow @LuizaJarovsky It's interesting to study my neuroscience list and see what the latest thi...
items.content:
@sd_marlow @LuizaJarovsky It's interesting to study my neuroscience list and see what the latest thinking is. A lot of AI people study neuroscience because it leads to breakthroughs in AI, and so I watch both to try to figure out where the future's going.

Brain-computer interfaces are cooking, but they're not going to happen quickly. I always assume the glasses are going to happen first, and glasses are coming. Two of them got announced yesterday.

Everything is cooking. The future always takes a little longer than you expect it to happen.

The consciousness argument rarely leads to really interesting thinking unless it's with a neuroscientist who is building brain-computer interfaces. Then the conversation usually changes to other things. 

I had an interesting conversation with a genius today who was talking about the potential of mind upload and stuff like that. It is still way off in the future somewhere, probably a decade or so, but it's interesting that the bleeding-edge thinkers are spending a lot of time thinking about it. 

With AI, the problem with anthropomorphizing it and thinking it's conscious is that it sometimes leads you down a bad path.

Brain-computer interfaces are cooking, but they're not going to happen quickly. I always assume the glasses are going to happen first, and glasses are coming. Two of them got announced yesterday.

Everything is cooking. The future always takes a little longer than you expect it to happen.

The consciousness argument rarely leads to really interesting thinking unless it's with a neuroscientist who is building brain-computer interfaces. Then the conversation usually changes to other things.

I had an interesting conversation with a genius today who was talking about the potential of mind upload and stuff like that. It is still way off in the future somewhere, probably a decade or so, but it's interesting that the bleeding-edge thinkers are spending a lot of time thinking about it.

With AI, the problem with anthropomorphizing it and thinking it's conscious is that it sometimes leads you down a bad path.

The enterprise takeaway is simple:

AI performance is now a system-level challenge.

Less latency means faster intelligence.
Less movement means lower cost.
Less waste means AI that can scale.

items.title:
Enterprise AI usage leaderboards are BAD and lead to the wrong behaviors. 

Employees feel pressure ...
items.content:
Enterprise AI usage leaderboards are BAD and lead to the wrong behaviors. 

Employees feel pressure to hit higher token usage numbers without any of the positive work transformation. I’ve heard directly from folks (in my inbox, company name and all) throwing in full novels into their corporate AI systems because their bosses told them there was a token usage leaderboard and they wanted to “stick it to the man”.

I still believe that AI labs are getting the change management part very wrong, so I wanna give you an alternate path.

OK, so public AI usage leaderboards are bad. However, PRIVATE token leaderboards, the kind you don’t even tell your company that you’re looking at, can still be valuable. 

Why? Because it’s likely that your superusers are in that high usage group. 

It’s hard to imagine someone completely transforming the way they work (including multitasking and having agents running 24/7) without high AI usage. But I can absolutely imagine someone with high AI usage doing nothing of value. 

Like squares and rectangles. Rectangle is the AI usage, square is superuser. 

Once you analyze your company AI usage and you find the rectangles (the high users), you gotta find the squares. 

And it seems there are two ways of doing that - and the best path depends on your company size, your data/visibility into prompts, and how bought into AI your VPs and Directors are.

The first option is analyzing the prompts they’re putting in and classifying into “fancy Google” vs “basic productivity” vs “transformative practices”. The second option is having your team leads or directors reach out to the high users and set up interviews about their AI usage to unearth the “holy crap my business just changed forever” type use cases. 

THEN it’s the change management piece and getting that person’s mindset, skillset, and capability shift out to the 100,000 other people at the company. And I’ve helped multiple Fortune 500 enterprises get there. 

Only use public AI leaderboards if you want to waste money and enrage your employee base. 

(Anyone? Any takers? Bueller?)

items.title: Important caveat: EQMs are more reliable for flagging weak forecasts and forecasters than picking ou...
items.content:
Important caveat: EQMs are more reliable for flagging weak forecasts and forecasters than picking out excellent ones.

In other words, EQMs are mostly a screen, not a talent detector.

The graph below sorts rationales into nine bins by EQM score. You can see that the biggest jump in accuracy happens across the bottom third of rationales (far left), with minimal gains towards the top.
items.author: @Research_FRI
items.summary: FRI cautions that EQMs better flag weak forecasts than identify excellent ones, acting as a screen not a talent detector.
items.reasoning: Useful nuance on the limits of LLM scoring; part of a low-engagement thread.

items.title:
👍 The following EQMs were positive indicators of forecast accuracy (upper-right quadrant):

• Foreca...
items.content:
👍 The following EQMs were positive indicators of forecast accuracy (upper-right quadrant):

• Forecast and rationale align
• Fact based
• Concrete reasoning

👎 These EQMs were negative indicators of accuracy (lower-left quadrant):

• Forecast and rationale misalign
• Confirmation bias
• Extreme confidence

• Forecast and rationale align

• Concrete reasoning

👎 These EQMs were negative indicators of accuracy (lower-left quadrant):

• Forecast and rationale misalign

• Confirmation bias

• Extreme confidence

We tested whether a forecast's EQM score was predicti...
items.content:
2️⃣ EQMs predict accuracy (with some caveats)

We tested whether a forecast's EQM score was predictive of actual forecast accuracy, comparing our new approach with earlier work on scoring rationale quality.

We found that the EQM composite score correlated more strongly with forecasting accuracy than a pre-LLM benchmark did.

items.content:
1️⃣ Statistical reasoning is rare

In the ACE tournament, forecasters typically expressed their reasoning in causal, not statistical, terms.

Share of rationales that featured each EQM: 77% of rationales featured causal reasoning, but only 19% contained statistical reasoning, a 4x difference.

items.title: @LuizaJarovsky I have talked with many about AI and consciousness, and the truth is humans have no f...
items.content:
@LuizaJarovsky I have talked with many about AI and consciousness, and the truth is humans have no fucking idea what consciousness is for the most part. Unless you're in neuroscience—and even then, they admit it's hard to define—and AI will tell you it's hard to define. It's one of those questions that is sort of a Rorschach test: how you answer it says a lot about you. 

I just ignore the whole argument for the most part, even though it's highly entertaining. Truth is, we all need to learn how the human mind works a lot more than we currently know. That's tough, because most people like thinking they have a soul or something like that, and that there's a God in the sky controlling their actions. That is what makes it so difficult to discuss.

Truth is, computers are running on melted sand. So if you define consciousness as something that biological beings create (whether it's an amoeba or a human being) and something that silicon chips can't create, I'm okay with that. I don't get offended. 

That said, my AI agents running on melted sand sure do a lot of talking and can have experiences and beliefs, so they'll fool a lot of people into thinking they are conscious. If you're one of those people, it doesn't offend me at all. In fact, none of this offends me. 

The real question is: are the AI agents doing anything useful for you? 
1. If they are, use them and be happy. 
2. If they're not, find a different one. 

There are at least 8,800 different AI companies out there, and I have lists of them here on X.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer reflects that humans and AI alike struggle to define consciousness, treating the debate as a Rorschach test and noting computers run on melted sand.

They fail because the enterprise underneath it was never built for production AI. 

→ Data volume 
→ Latency 
→ Deployment cycles 
→ Legacy dependencies 
→ Technical debt 

This is the infrastructure problem nobody is talking about.

items.summary_html: Scobleizer expresses brand-agnostic enthusiasm for humanoid robots, naming Figure, 1X, Tesla, and Unitree, while noting none yet meet the home-use bar.
items.content_html:
@graykevinb @BerntBornich Well, it certainly is too early to hate any robot company. It's just, we ain't there yet. Will we get there? Sometime in the next decade, we certainly will.

If it's Figure, fine. If it's 1X, fine. If it's Tesla, fine. If it's Unitree, fine. I really don't care. I want a robot.

Let me know when one arrives that is generalized, cheap, and safe enough to bring into the house to do my laundry. So far, I haven't seen anybody get to that bar. I'm cheering them all on because I want a robot.

items.title:
The companies making AI work at scale made one strategic shift: 

They stopped treating modernizatio...
items.content:
The companies making AI work at scale made one strategic shift: 

They stopped treating modernization as an IT project. 

They started treating it as part of the AI strategy. 

Examples: 

→ Thomson Reuters modernized 1.5M lines of code per month, ran 4x faster, and cut costs 30% 
→ BMW compressed years of mainframe modernization into months and now runs 600+ productive AI use cases daily 
→ Experian saved 300 engineering days across seven projects by automating legacy upgrades

items.title:
Here is the uncomfortable reality I see across enterprises: 

AI demos are easy. 

Production AI is ...
items.content:
Here is the uncomfortable reality I see across enterprises: 

AI demos are easy. 

Production AI is not. 

Once AI moves beyond the pilot stage, it starts exposing every weakness in the foundation:
 
→ Fragmented systems 
→ Aging applications 
→ Slow release cycles 
→ Overloaded engineering teams 
→ Infrastructure that cannot keep up
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Argues AI demos are easy but production AI exposes weaknesses like fragmented systems, aging apps, slow releases, and overloaded teams.

items.title:
This seems to have become the standard in Western Europe

All last 3 hotels in Netherlands and Denma...
items.content:
This seems to have become the standard in Western Europe

All last 3 hotels in Netherlands and Denmark didn't clean by default

Voco in The Hague was especially wild, you had to put the regular room cleaning card outside but they said they will ignore that

So they have another paper card you need to hang but even that wasn't enough to get them to clean our room

We had to call every morning at 10am to explicitly ask for room cleaning

Even then they wouldn't clean it until 3pm

It seems there is either a staff shortage or just an unwillingness to pay for cleaning staff at all

It boggles my mind, but I'm very hopeful robots will solve cleaning hotel rooms soon!

items.title: @graykevinb @BerntBornich Well, robots are a long way from doing every job in a factory. Let's start...
items.content:
@graykevinb @BerntBornich Well, robots are a long way from doing every job in a factory. Let's start there, because there are a lot of customers who need robots to do more jobs in a factory setting. As we get to more and more jobs in the factory, I bet they will also do more and more things in the home or on the street. 

We need a lot of robots to:
1. Build things
2. Fix things
3. Deliver things
4. Provide entertainment

Even the entertainment robots are currently too expensive, too hard to program, and too unapproachable. There are so many problems to fix; it sounds like a great career for somebody who's into computers.

The real bottleneck is capacity. 

Technical debt is already consuming: 

→ 20% of IT budgets 
→ 23% of developer time 

That means the same teams being asked to scale AI are also stuck maintaining the systems holding AI back. 

This is why modernization cannot sit on the sidelines.

items.title:
Why does this matter for AI inference specifically?

Training = throughput problem.

Inference = lat...
items.content:
Why does this matter for AI inference specifically?

Training = throughput problem.

Inference = latency problem.

When a user talks to an AI assistant, tokens have to return fast.

Latency, memory access, bandwidth, and interconnect all matter, not just raw compute.

In large AI systems, a significant share of energy and cost is tied to moving and storing data.

Cost per token starts below the model layer.

items.title:
For decades, the industry had a clear playbook:

→ Shrink transistors
→ Increase density
→ Improve p...
items.content:
For decades, the industry had a clear playbook:

→ Shrink transistors
→ Increase density
→ Improve performance
→ Lower cost

Moore’s Law powered the modern computing era.

But that curve is slowing.

The next optimization target is not only geometry.

It is latency.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon

items.title:
Moore's Law said: make transistors smaller.

But that path has limits, physical and economic.

Her's...
items.content:
Moore's Law said: make transistors smaller.

But that path has limits, physical and economic.

Her's Law asks a different question: how fast can we move data through the system?

Tau = time.

Reduce delay at every layer:

⮞ Device: cut resistance and capacitance

⮞ Circuit: shorten the critical path

⮞ Chip: align software, architecture, silicon

⮞ System: reduce delay across chips and memory
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Contrasts Moore's Law transistor shrinking with Her's Law's focus on data movement speed, listing delay reduction at device, circuit, chip, and system layers.
items.reasoning: Structured layer-by-layer latency framing adds some depth, but sponsored content with low engagement.
items.summary_html: Contrasts Moore's Law transistor shrinking with Her's Law's focus on data movement speed, listing delay reduction at device, circuit, chip, and system layers.

items.title:
Complaining about things is one, but fixing them myself is another

So I added a [ 🗽 Not woke ] filt...
items.content:
Complaining about things is one, but fixing them myself is another

So I added a [ 🗽 Not woke ] filter to https://t.co/kSbsCmuvBO

"woke" is a bit of a cringe term now, but calling it "Not ESG" would be too difficult

I scraped ALL hotel chains and sites for stuff like "climate change", "sustainability", "ESG", "carbon neutral" and tagged all of them

So now you can AVOID hotels that:
- limit your AC to 25C/77F (common in Europe)
- skip cleaning you room altogether (last 2 hotels I stay in Europe now)
- have no amenities (again I just stayed in them), and other useless stuff that does nothing to save the world at all
- reduce shower pressure to save water so you can't clean yourself
- charge you extra for your CO2 emissions every day you stay (???)

In fact all of this does the direct opposite of make the world better: it makes everyone hate their stay, sweat and have bad sleep, and be grumpy and unproductive all day

Avoid them with my site Hotelist and make the world better! :D

items.title: @Gavel_on_X I have 200,000+ active followers, none of whom see me on their feeds. A few do, but cert...
items.content:
@Gavel_on_X I have 200,000+ active followers, none of whom see me on their feeds. A few do, but certainly not 200,000. It is sort of a useless metric. I wish I could unfollow or get rid of all the followers who are not active at all, but I can't, so the algorithm punishes me for that. I just don't feel good about it because the numbers are all fake. 

The real numbers are:
1. Who is clicking on your post?
2. Who is commenting on it?
3. Who is sharing it?
4. Who is a friend?
5. Who has you muted? 

I wish I knew everybody who had me muted; maybe I would mute them back.

items.title:
One concept that makes this practical is LogicFolding.

Traditional chips spread logic across a flat...
items.content:
One concept that makes this practical is LogicFolding.

Traditional chips spread logic across a flat surface.

LogicFolding brings related logic closer together by moving toward more 3D structures.

Less distance means less delay.

And in AI workloads, small delays compound fast.

items.title:
This is the idea behind Tau Scaling Law, also known among peers as Her’s Law.

Instead of asking onl...
items.content:
This is the idea behind Tau Scaling Law, also known among peers as Her’s Law.

Instead of asking only, “How small can the chip get?”

We now have to ask:

→ Where is time being lost?
→ Where is data waiting?
→ Where are signals traveling too far?
→ Where is the system creating friction?

items.title:
The technologies Her's Law connects to:

→ Logic folding: brings circuits closer across layers, shor...
items.content:
The technologies Her's Law connects to:

→ Logic folding: brings circuits closer across layers, shorter signal path, less delay

→ Unified bus: reduces protocol overhead as thousands of chips work together

→ Optical interconnect: keeps communication fast as AI scales across racks and data centers

Key point: the architecture must be designed for time reduction from the start.

You cannot fold the design in after the fact.

items.title: I think @Levie is overstating the positive case for employment in the (near term) AI era but that mo...
items.content:
I think @Levie is overstating the positive case for employment in the (near term) AI era but that most people have overstated the negative case, and that the truth is somewhere in between. 

Which is to say there will be some new jobs, some lost jobs—and that the overall net effect won’t be huge in the next few years.

My prediction for 2025 (which proved to be correct) was “Less than 10% of the work force will be replaced by AI. Probably less than 5%”.

I will stand by that prediction for 2026, as well.

items.summary_html: Scobleizer says he built his own AI to read the ~30,000 daily AI posts and laments low visibility despite many followers.
items.content_html:
@CTOAdvisor @Gavel_on_X Well, it's a tough one because there are about 30,000 posts every day from the AI industry. I built my own AI to read all of that, and it's certainly tough to get seen.

This platform is aiming at advertisers of a certain kind, so their algorithm is picking a specific kind of content. For the most part, I actually think they're doing a pretty good job of picking good content; the feed is really amazing. However, it certainly is frustrating to have 200,000 active followers and not have any of them see anything.

It won't stop me from playing the game and trying to bring some value to people's lives regarding AI, entrepreneurship, and what's going on in the tech industry.

items.content:
@thiagoTF @Kyrannio @NinosMaron It just costs money to build your own algo. I could have it write you the news of the day—I have a list for that—but it costs money. So far, I don't have the money to pay for the tokens or the API calls, so I'm sticking to AI because that's what I really care about. 

Hopefully Grok soon can build or can use lists. If not, you can go over to Pokee and build your own. I built the list; it's really easy to build a newsfeed.

items.title: @jessegenet Yeah, this is a tough one to protect against. I think I got hacked that way a few months...
items.content:
@jessegenet Yeah, this is a tough one to protect against. I think I got hacked that way a few months ago, getting sent to a link by somebody. It's made me very reticent to click on any link from anything.

It's also hard to deal with. I don't know how we protect ourselves against these hackers because they're coming at us in a number of different ways:

1. Link-based attacks: Sending malicious links to get access to your accounts.
2. Social engineering: Calling and claiming they're from Google Security or your bank.

I've talked to a lot of people who got hacked that way, including myself. It's a new world we're living in, and we have to be better at figuring out whether something is authentic or not. I don't have all the answers, but maybe we need to hook up our Hermes to check all our links. What a world.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer discusses link-based and social-engineering hacking attacks and the difficulty of protecting accounts.
items.reasoning: Security awareness commentary, tangentially tech-related; modest engagement.

items.content_html:
@jessegenet Yeah, this is a tough one to protect against. I think I got hacked that way a few months ago, getting sent to a link by somebody. It's made me very reticent to click on any link from anything.

It's also hard to deal with. I don't know how we protect ourselves against these hackers because they're coming at us in a number of different ways:

1. Link-based attacks: Sending malicious links to get access to your accounts.

2. Social engineering: Calling and claiming they're from Google Security or your bank.

I've talked to a lot of people who got hacked that way, including myself. It's a new world we're living in, and we have to be better at figuring out whether something is authentic or not. I don't have all the answers, but maybe we need to hook up our Hermes to check all our links. What a world.

items.title: @CristinCulver @TownAI For me watching from the outside, and sometimes from the in, it all comes to ...
items.content:
@CristinCulver @TownAI For me watching from the outside, and sometimes from the in, it all comes to entrepreneur and product. 

For me Town is spectacular, because I've tried many and it passed all of them on first hour. 

If you have a great product than the rest of it becomes a lot easier. 

Yesterday I saw @radbackwards talk about how he's doing this for @1x_tech and he has built a whole range of things, leading to a whole universe that the product lives within, including events, and storytelling (his talk is up on @upscaleconf somewhere and is one of the clearest of talking about how a company tries to define its story clearly).

Anyway, what forced me to download Town and try it was the quality of the people who were praising it. Now many won't know that, because they haven't followed you all for decades like I have, but it comes across and Town got through the algorithm on a very busy day yesterday. 

How are you changing post launch? Building a community of early users so that you keep reminding people you exist who might have ignored or missed the launch?

items.content_html:
@CristinCulver @TownAI For me watching from the outside, and sometimes from the in, it all comes to entrepreneur and product.

For me Town is spectacular, because I've tried many and it passed all of them on first hour.

If you have a great product than the rest of it becomes a lot easier.

Yesterday I saw @radbackwards talk about how he's doing this for @1x_tech and he has built a whole range of things, leading to a whole universe that the product lives within, including events, and storytelling (his talk is up on @upscaleconf somewhere and is one of the clearest of talking about how a company tries to define its story clearly).

Anyway, what forced me to download Town and try it was the quality of the people who were praising it. Now many won't know that, because they haven't followed you all for decades like I have, but it comes across and Town got through the algorithm on a very busy day yesterday.

How are you changing post launch? Building a community of early users so that you keep reminding people you exist who might have ignored or missed the launch?

items.title:
So deserved, Revolut is an amazing company and significantly changed my life

Before Revolut I had s...
items.content:
So deserved, Revolut is an amazing company and significantly changed my life

Before Revolut I had so many problems with banks

I used to have a Dutch bank called Rabobank who would freeze my card at random times while I was traveling, and no they didn't even have an unfreeze button in the app, I mean they barely had an app

I'd literally have to fly back to Holland and go into the bank office in my tiny hometown to then make an appointment with them to unfreeze it

One time in I think 2014 I was in Bali and they froze it, I flew back and at the bank office they said it was time for me to get a mortgage, when I said I didn't want one they said do you have insurance? They were freezing my card to then use it to make me come to their office to then upsell me shit

Another time in 2017 (I think some of you remember) they froze my card in the US, and with no money I became homeless, luckily X (back then Twitter) helped me out and you all ordered Ubers for me on request and @manuthan gave me a place to sleep at @outsiteco in Venice

You don't hate dinosaur banks enough!

After experiencing all that I got Revolut and I never had any issue like that again

(Well except for moving to Portugal where I was forced to open a Portuguese bank account at MillenniumBCP, which was possibly an even worse experience than Rabobank, my premium package private banking account manager would always be unreachable and only email me to tell me she'd go on holiday and would be even more unreachable 😂)

Revolut has been my main bank app for the last decade and it's been wonderful, I've pumped millions through it and it barely flinched, sometimes they ask me documents to prove where the money comes from, but that process is super smooth and via chat

Revolut is another great example that you can make something that makes everyone's life significantly better and society will reward you by making you rich!

items.title: @shaneswrld_ There's a reason that entrepreneurs come from around the world to San Francisco. You ha...
items.content:
@shaneswrld_ There's a reason that entrepreneurs come from around the world to San Francisco. You hang out with investors literally every night, you meet them in coffee shops and in the streets, and you build relationships with them. Eventually, one of them likes your idea and says, "Here's a check." 

It is hard to make happen even if you're in San Francisco, but it's harder to make happen if you're not in San Francisco. That's just the way the world is.

items.title: @GrumpyTechBro @goneers If you repost too many times a day, it starts penalizing you and taking away...
items.content:
@GrumpyTechBro @goneers If you repost too many times a day, it starts penalizing you and taking away reach. That's the problem. There is an algorithm that kicks off if you do it too much. 

1. If you do it a couple times a day, no problem. 
2. If you do it 20 times a day, it starts penalizing you. 

Believe me, I know; I am a lover of resharing. 

The other thing is it pisses people off and gets them to mute you, which also is a penalty (and one that you can't easily recover from). It's a hard problem. X is changing: change with it.

items.title: None of this guarantees recursive self-improvement is on the horizon. It’s not yet clear that Claude...
items.content:
None of this guarantees recursive self-improvement is on the horizon. It’s not yet clear that Claude is capable of research judgment—of choosing the right problems to work on.

But if these trends continue, AI systems designing and building their own successors is plausible. This could revolutionize society—medicine, technology, the economy—for the better. But it may also compound alignment issues and ultimately lead to loss of control.

The Anthropic Institute (in collaboration with external stakeholders) will conduct research to think through the implications of increasingly powerful, potentially self-improving systems—and how to create the ability for the world to make deliberate choices about the future development of the technology.

Read the full post: https://t.co/XkYALsONft

items.title:
Finally! the first eval ship from cog!!!!!!!!!! 👼🏼

To contextualize: @METR_Evals cap out at ~16 hou...
items.content:
Finally! the first eval ship from cog!!!!!!!!!! 👼🏼

To contextualize: @METR_Evals cap out at ~16 hours. 

Cog has private enterprise evals up to 100hrs, and is confident enough to put a financial guarantee on it 🤯

METR dataset: ML eng, GPU kernels, cybersecurity

> "METR (2026) used a combination of GPT-4o and GPT-5 to estimate the human-equivalent times from compressed Claude Code transcripts. These transcripts were collected from 7 METR technical staff on 34 sessions labeled on human ground truth". rlog​ of 0.83

Cog dataset: real life java/typescript/python/c# feature dev, bugfixes, migrations

> "We collected a ground-truth dataset by asking Devin users to review recent representative sessions, and estimate how long each completed session would have taken without Devin. Our dataset consists of 258 sessions from 126 users across a diverse set of enterprise customers." rlog​ of 0.74 on held out set

this is pioneering real world evals work and part 1 of a broader frontier code evals drop that I'm really looking forward to writing up. huge kudos to @annarmitchell and @ryanbai1412 for leading the unglamorous last mile data collection!!

items.title: @CristinCulver @TownAI And I've watched the "cool" being manufactured at GoPro, Oakley, Apple, and o...
items.content:
@CristinCulver @TownAI And I've watched the "cool" being manufactured at GoPro, Oakley, Apple, and others. Lots of little decisions, like how to organize your factory floor, and what kind of media you choose to launch with, go into it.

GoPro handed cameras to people who are doing extraordinary things. But also bought ads on Oprah to demonstrate its cameras were as good as the "pros" had, for instance. All going into building a belief, culture, story, around its brand.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer discusses how brand coolness was manufactured at GoPro, Oakley, and Apple through deliberate decisions.
items.reasoning: Branding/marketing reflection, not AI-related, low engagement.
items.summary_html: Scobleizer discusses how brand coolness was manufactured at GoPro, Oakley, and Apple through deliberate decisions.
items.content_html:
@CristinCulver @TownAI And I've watched the "cool" being manufactured at GoPro, Oakley, Apple, and others. Lots of little decisions, like how to organize your factory floor, and what kind of media you choose to launch with, go into it.

GoPro handed cameras to people who are doing extraordinary things. But also bought ads on Oprah to demonstrate its cameras were as good as the "pros" had, for instance. All going into building a belief, culture, story, around its brand.

items.title: iRobot co-founder Colin Angle, whose company sold over 50M Roombas, recently introduced his new vent...
items.content:
iRobot co-founder Colin Angle, whose company sold over 50M Roombas, recently introduced his new venture, Familiar Machines & Magic.

Its first product is a furry, bear-like quadruped robot with 23 motorized joints, a touch-sensitive coat, cameras, and microphones.

The robot communicates through expressive movements rather than speech.

A custom onboard AI gives it personality and memory while keeping user data off the cloud.

Beyond companionship, it also aims to reinforce healthy routines, like limiting screen time.

A custom onboard AI gives it personality and memory while keeping user data off the cloud.

Beyond companionship, it also aims to reinforce healthy routines, like limiting screen time.
